title: Pramāṇavārttikakārikā
author: Dharmakīrti
tradition: Buddhist (pramāṇavāda / Dignāga–Dharmakīrti epistemological school, with Yogācāra–Sautrāntika commitments)
summary: The verse-text (kārikā) of Dharmakīrti's magnum opus on valid cognition (pramāṇa), comprising roughly 2,000 stanzas in four chapters: inference-for-oneself (svārthānumāna), establishment of the means of knowledge / the Buddha as authority (pramāṇasiddhi), perception (pratyakṣa), and inference-for-others / proof (parārthānumāna). It defines perception as non-conceptual and non-erroneous and inference as grounded in necessary (causal or identity) relations.
history: Composed as an expansion of and commentary on Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya, the work became the foundational text of Buddhist epistemology and generated a vast commentarial tradition in India and Tibet. The kārikā verses are often transmitted and studied separately from Dharmakīrti's own and others' prose commentaries.

title: Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti [Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti]
author: Dharmakīrti
summary: Dharmakīrti's own prose autocommentary (svavṛtti) on the first chapter of the Pramāṇavārttika, the chapter on inference-for-oneself (svārthānumāna). It expounds in detail his theories of the logical reason (hetu), necessary relations (vyāpti), the apoha (exclusion) theory of meaning and concept-formation, and the refutation of rival semantic and inferential theories.
history: Dharmakīrti wrote a self-commentary only on the svārthānumāna chapter; the surviving Sanskrit was edited by Gnoli (1960). It is among the most studied Sanskrit sources for his philosophy of language and was elaborated by Śākyabuddhi and Karṇakagomin.

title: Sambandhaparīkṣā
author: Dharmakīrti
summary: A short treatise (about 25 verses with Dharmakīrti's own vṛtti) examining the notion of 'relation' (sambandha). Dharmakīrti argues that real relations such as conjunction or inherence cannot exist between distinct momentary entities, so relations are conceptual constructions superimposed on causally connected particulars.
history: One of Dharmakīrti's seven treatises (the 'seven works on pramāṇa'), it survives in Sanskrit fragments and Tibetan translation and was commented on by Vinītadeva and others. It is important for Buddhist nominalism about relations and the critique of Nyāya–Vaiśeṣika categories.

title: Vādanyāya
author: Dharmakīrti
genre: philosophical treatise on debate (vāda) and dialectics
summary: A treatise on the rules and logic of formal philosophical debate, defining the legitimate grounds for defeat (nigrahasthāna) in disputation. Dharmakīrti criticizes the Nyāya analysis of debate, reducing valid points of defeat essentially to stating a non-establishing reason or failing to state a sound one, and rejects many of the traditional Naiyāyika categories of defeat.
history: The last of Dharmakīrti's seven pramāṇa works, it survives in Sanskrit and was commented on by Vinītadeva and Śāntarakṣita (Vipañcitārthā). It is a key source for Buddhist debate theory and the polemic against the Nyāyasūtra tradition.

title: Nyāyabinduprakaraṇa
author: Dharmakīrti
genre: philosophical treatise / manual (prakaraṇa) on logic and epistemology
summary: A compact prose manual ('A Drop of Logic') in three chapters covering perception (pratyakṣa), inference-for-oneself (svārthānumāna), and inference-for-others (parārthānumāna). It distills Dharmakīrti's epistemology into an accessible introductory form, defining valid cognition, the three marks of a sound logical reason, and the types of fallacies.
history: One of the most widely studied of Dharmakīrti's seven works, it survives complete in Sanskrit and is famous through Dharmottara's commentary (Nyāyabinduṭīkā) and Stcherbatsky's English translation in 'Buddhist Logic'. It served as a standard introduction to Buddhist logic.

title: Hetutattvopadeśa
author: Jitāri (Jetāri)
tradition: Buddhist (Dignāga–Dharmakīrti epistemological school; classed by Tibetans as Yogācāra-Svātantrika-Madhyamaka)
date_estimate: c. 940-1000 CE (latter half of the 10th c.)
summary: A short instructional treatise ('Teaching on the True Nature of the Logical Reason') on the theory of the valid inferential reason (hetu) and its threefold characteristic, together with the types of correct and fallacious reasons. It is a concise pedagogical text within the post-Dharmakīrti logical tradition.
history: Jitāri was a prolific Buddhist scholar at Vikramaśīla and a teacher of Atiśa. The Hetutattvopadeśa survives in Sanskrit and Tibetan and was used as an introductory logic primer; multiple recensions/editions exist (hence the two work-IDs in this batch).

title: Hetutattvopadeśa
author: Jitāri (Jetāri)
tradition: Buddhist (Dignāga–Dharmakīrti epistemological school; classed by Tibetans as Yogācāra-Svātantrika-Madhyamaka)
date_estimate: c. 940-1000 CE (latter half of the 10th c.)
summary: A duplicate record (alternate edition/recension) of Jitāri's 'Teaching on the True Nature of the Logical Reason', a concise manual on the threefold mark of the valid inferential reason and the classification of sound and fallacious reasons in the Dharmakīrtian tradition.
history: See the companion entry (T11jithettu): Jitāri was a 10th-century Vikramaśīla scholar and teacher of Atiśa; this work is a short logic primer surviving in Sanskrit and Tibetan. The two IDs likely reflect two editions of the same text.

title: Nibandhāvalī
author: Jñānaśrīmitra
tradition: Buddhist (Yogācāra and pramāṇavāda / late Indian epistemological school)
summary: The 'Garland of Treatises' is the collected corpus of Jñānaśrīmitra's philosophical monographs, including works on momentariness (Kṣaṇabhaṅgādhyāya), exclusion/apoha (Apohaprakaraṇa), pervasion (Vyāpticarcā), non-perception (Anupalabdhirahasya), the means of valid cognition, and the reality of the external world. The essays defend Dharmakīrtian positions against Nyāya and Mīmāṃsā critics.
history: Jñānaśrīmitra was a leading scholar at Vikramaśīla and teacher of Ratnakīrti. The Nibandhāvalī was edited by Anantalal Thakur (Tibetan Sanskrit Works Series, Patna, 1959) as a single collection of his Buddhist nyāya works; it is a principal source for late Indian Buddhist epistemology.

title: Pramāṇavārttikavṛtti Commentary [Pramāṇavārttikavṛttiṭīkā]
author: Karṇakagomin
summary: A detailed Sanskrit sub-commentary on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti (the autocommentary on the first, svārthānumāna chapter). It closely follows and expands Śākyabuddhi's commentary, explicating Dharmakīrti's logic, the apoha theory, and his arguments against rival schools.
history: Karṇakagomin's ṭīkā survives in Sanskrit (edited by Rāhula Sāṅkṛtyāyana) and is, with Śākyabuddhi, a crucial witness for reconstructing the text of the Pramāṇavārttikasvavṛtti. Karṇakagomin is conventionally placed in the 8th-9th century; the '40-120 CE' figure seen in some web sources is erroneous.

title: Vādanyāyaṭīkā
author: Vinītadeva
summary: Vinītadeva's commentary on Dharmakīrti's Vādanyāya, explicating the rules of debate and the grounds of defeat (nigrahasthāna) and clarifying Dharmakīrti's critique of the Nyāya theory of disputation.
history: Vinītadeva was a prolific commentator who annotated several of Dharmakīrti's seven works as well as Vasubandhu, Sthiramati, and Dignāga. Most of his commentaries survive primarily in Tibetan translation. The work-ID number (1226) corresponds to the Bibliotheca Indica 'New Series No. 1226' provenance used by this project.

title: Jātinirākṛti
author: anonymous/traditional (attributed in some editions to Dharmakīrti or his school)
summary: A short polemical tract, 'Refutation of the Universal' (jāti), arguing against the realist (Nyāya–Vaiśeṣika and Mīmāṃsā) doctrine of real, eternal universals (sāmānya/jāti). In line with the apoha theory it holds that words and concepts do not denote positive universals but operate by exclusion of what is other.
history: The text is one of the 'Six Buddhist Nyāya Tracts' edited by Haraprasad Shastri (Bibliotheca Indica, 1910). Its authorship is uncertain — it is sometimes ascribed to Dharmakīrti or treated as a school product; hence it is best given as anonymous within the Dharmakīrtian tradition.
related_works: Six Buddhist Nyaya Tracts (ed. Haraprasad Shastri)

title: Nyāyabinduṭīkāṭippaṇī
author: anonymous
date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (after Dharmottara, fl. c. 740-800)
summary: An anonymous set of glosses (ṭippaṇī) on Dharmottara's Nyāyabinduṭīkā, which is itself a commentary on Dharmakīrti's Nyāyabindu. It clarifies points of Dharmottara's exposition of perception, inference, and logical reasons.
history: The text was edited by Th. Stcherbatsky and published in the Bibliotheca Buddhica series (1909). It belongs to the layered commentarial tradition on the Nyāyabindu (Dharmakīrti → Dharmottara → ṭippaṇī).

title: Bauddha manīṣā - Bauddheṣu Pramāṇamīmāṃsā
genre: modern scholarly study / anthology on Buddhist epistemology (likely)
date_estimate: modern (20th c.) — uncertain; title not securely identified
summary: The title translates roughly as 'Buddhist Thought — Investigation of the Means of Knowledge among the Buddhists', i.e. a treatment of pramāṇa (epistemology) in the Buddhist tradition. It appears to be a modern Sanskrit-medium study or anthology surveying Buddhist theories of valid cognition (perception and inference) rather than a single classical work.
history: Could not be securely identified with a specific classical text or named author in available sources; the phrasing ('Bauddha manīṣā') is characteristic of modern Sanskrit secondary literature on Indian philosophy. Treated as low confidence pending a positive catalogue identification.

title: Anupalabdhirahasya
author: Jñānaśrīmitra
tradition: Buddhist (Yogācāra and pramāṇavāda / late Indian epistemological school)
genre: philosophical treatise (prakaraṇa, part of the Nibandhāvalī)
summary: The 'Secret of Non-Perception' is a treatise on anupalabdhi — the inference of the absence (abhāva) of a perceptible thing from its non-perception. Jñānaśrīmitra refines Dharmakīrti's theory of how non-cognition can serve as a valid means of knowing absence, against rival accounts of negation.
history: One of the monographs forming Jñānaśrīmitra's Nibandhāvalī. It was critically edited and studied (with the Sarvaśabdābhāvacarcā) by Birgit Kellner, who surveyed his anupalabdhi-theory. It is a major late-Buddhist contribution to the epistemology of negation.

title: Viśālāmalavatī Pramāṇasamuccayaṭīkā
author: Jinendrabuddhi
date_estimate: c. 2nd half of the 8th c. CE (Jinendrabuddhi, c. 710-770)
summary: The 'Spotless and Extensive' commentary on Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya (with its vṛtti), explicating Dignāga's foundational treatise on perception and inference. It is the only known commentary on the Pramāṇasamuccaya and a vital aid to reconstructing Dignāga's otherwise Sanskrit-lost text.
history: Jinendrabuddhi was an 8th-century scholar; his ṭīkā has been recovered in Sanskrit manuscripts and critically edited (Chapters 1-2) in the Sanskrit Texts from the Tibetan Autonomous Region series by Steinkellner, Krasser, Lasic, and others. It is indispensable for the study of Dignāga.

title: Nyāyapraveśakasūtra
author: Śaṅkarasvāmin (traditionally; sometimes ascribed to Dignāga)
date_estimate: c. 6th c. CE (after Dignāga, before Dharmakīrti)
summary: A brief 'Introduction to Logic' presenting the core elements of Dignāga's system: the members of a proof (thesis, reason, example), the three characteristics of a valid reason, the fallacies of thesis, reason, and example, perception and inference, and refutation. It is a compact primer for engaging in Buddhist logical debate.
history: Most commonly attributed to Dignāga's pupil Śaṅkarasvāmin, though the Tibetan colophon and some scholars assign it to Dignāga himself. Extremely influential in East Asia (as the Yinming ru zhengli lun, basis of the Chinese/Japanese yinming/inmyō logic tradition) and preserved in Sanskrit, Chinese, and Tibetan.

title: Pramāṇavārttika
author: Dharmakīrti
genre: philosophical treatise (verse epistemology, with autocommentary on ch. 1)
summary: Dharmakīrti's magnum opus, the 'Commentary on Valid Cognition', in four chapters: inference-for-oneself, establishment of valid cognition / the Buddha's authority, perception, and inference-for-others. It is the most comprehensive statement of Buddhist epistemology, logic, philosophy of language (apoha), and the proof of Buddhist soteriological claims.
history: Conceived as an expansion of Dignāga's Pramāṇasamuccaya, it became the central text of the Buddhist pramāṇa tradition and was commented on by Devendrabuddhi, Śākyabuddhi, Karṇakagomin, Prajñākaragupta, Manorathanandin, and many Tibetan authors. (This record likely denotes the whole work, complementing the separate kārikā and svavṛtti entries.)

title: Pramāṇavārttikālaṃkāra
author: Prajñākaragupta
summary: The 'Ornament of the Pramāṇavārttika', a vast commentary (over 16,000 ślokas) on Dharmakīrti's Pramāṇavārttika, beginning with the pramāṇasiddhi chapter. Prajñākaragupta develops a distinctive and sometimes innovative reading of Dharmakīrti, treating topics such as the proof of the Buddha as authority, causality, and momentariness.
history: Also called Pramāṇavārttikabhāṣya, it survives in a single Sanskrit manuscript and in Tibetan translation. Prajñākaragupta founded an influential interpretive lineage continued by Jayanta and Yamāri, and was studied closely by Jñānaśrīmitra and Ratnakīrti.

title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi Vyatirekātmikā
author: Ratnakīrti
tradition: Buddhist (Yogācāra and pramāṇavāda / late Indian epistemological school)
genre: philosophical treatise (prakaraṇa, part of the Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī)
summary: The 'Proof of Momentariness via the Negative Concomitance', one of two complementary proofs (the other being anvayātmikā, positive correlation) that Ratnakīrti gives for the doctrine that all existents are momentary (kṣaṇika). The vyatireka form argues that whatever is not momentary cannot have causal efficacy and hence cannot exist, defending universal momentariness against Nyāya critics.
history: Ratnakīrti, a pupil of Jñānaśrīmitra at Vikramaśīla, condensed and sharpened his teacher's arguments. The text was edited in the Six Buddhist Nyāya Tracts (Haraprasad Shastri) and the Ratnakīrtinibandhāvalī (A. Thakur); it was translated and studied by A. C. Senape McDermott.

title: Vādarahasya [Udayananirākaraṇa]
author: Ratnakīrti (attributed; uncertain)
date_estimate: c. 11th c. CE (if by Ratnakīrti); attribution uncertain
summary: A 'Secret of Debate', sub-titled here as a refutation of Udayana (Udayananirākaraṇa), i.e. a Buddhist rebuttal of the great Nyāya theist Udayana's arguments — particularly his proofs for God (Īśvara) and his critiques of Buddhist momentariness and idealism. The work defends Buddhist epistemological and ontological positions against Naiyāyika theism.
history: The attribution to Ratnakīrti is marked as uncertain in the project metadata and is debated; Udayana (fl. c. 1000 CE) was a contemporary or near-contemporary of Ratnakīrti, so a Buddhist refutation of him fits the late-11th-century Vikramaśīla milieu. Could not fully confirm the work's authorship from available sources, hence reduced confidence.
